_provenance: Verbatim chunks extracted from /docs (Arkiv.md, RabitQ.md, ERC.md, PHASES.md) by a corpus-builder subagent on 2026-05-25. Each gold/distractor is real project documentation text; trailing ' — <file> line N' markers are source citations stripped at load time. This is a REAL retrieval corpus (no synthetic/fabricated facts).

topics.query: What is the chain ID for the Braga testnet?
distractors: Block time: 2 seconds
RPC: https://braga.hoodi.arkiv.network/rpc
Effective max btl approximately 272 years (u32 cap on btl and expiresAt, 2-second blocks).
Real CREATE gas cost: ~29,000 gas (flat, no per-byte-per-block scaling)

topics.query: What address is the Arkiv registry precompile deployed at on Braga?
topics.gold: The on-chain registry is a Rust precompile at 0x00000000000000000000000000000061726b6976 ("arkiv" in ASCII) running inside op-reth.
distractors: 0x4400000000000000000000000000000000000044 (claimed predeploy in op-reth code) has 0 bytes
0x4200000000000000000000000000000000000044 (OP-Stack convention) is 2,059 bytes, an uninitialized Proxy.sol that reverts every call with 'Proxy: implementation not initialized'

topics.query: What calldata wire format does the Arkiv precompile accept?
topics.gold: Precompile accepts brotli-compressed RLP as calldata, not ABI.
distractors: The SDK's src/utils/arkivTransactions.ts confirms: compress(toRlp([creates, updates, deletes, extensions, ownershipChanges])).
This is the legacy 'golembase' wire format — Arkiv was previously called Golem DB.
Every eth_call with standard ABI selectors returns errors like 'brotli: PADDING_2', 'brotli: RESERVED', 'brotli: unexpected EOF'.
Wire format is brotli(RLP([creates, updates, deletes, extensions, ownershipChanges])), not ABI calldata execute(Operation[]) with selector 0xba8ccf92

topics.query: Why does naively extending an entity's lease revert, and what makes extend REPLACE not ADD?
topics.gold: Contract: newExpiresAt = currentBlock + btl, must be strictly greater than c.expiresAt. Source: EntityRegistry.sol:350-368.
distractors: requireExpiryIncreased(key, newExpiresAt, currentExpiresAt) reverts ExpiryNotExtended if newExpiresAt <= currentExpiresAt. This is what makes extend REPLACE-not-ADD.

If entity has 100 days left and you call extend(btl = 30 days), you lose the remaining 100 days — the new lease is just 30 days from now. You cannot stack leases. Each extension must beat the current expiry.
Extend is REPLACE not ADD, owner-only. newExpiresAt = currentBlock + btl, must be strictly greater than existingExpiresAt. Cannot stack leases. Cannot delegate.
requireActive(c) reverts if expired (expiresAt <= currentBlock). Strict-leq — entity is expired at exactly its expiresAt block.

topics.query: Can I recall expired entities using a historical atBlock query on Braga?
topics.gold: The server silently ignores the atBlock parameter. This may be unimplemented rather than buggy — but either way, the public API does not support historical recall today.
distractors: The SDK exposes validAtBlock(N: bigint) on the query builder.
query.validAtBlock(N) builder exists and serializes correctly — but server ignores it.
TestHistoricalQueries covers explicit-delete-then-query-at-past-block (works in tests, ignored on live Braga).
Test with atBlock = 0xFFFFFFFF (future) returns 'context cancelled'

topics.query: Who actually triggers EXPIRE events on Braga in practice?
topics.gold: Every observed EXPIRE on Braga came from the OP-Stack system depositor (0xdead...0001) via the L1Block sync path — not from third-party callers. The 'anyone can call EXPIRE for free permanence' loophole does not exist in practice.
distractors: The Arkiv precompile is hooked into the L1Block sync transaction. When each L2 block updates its L1 reference, the precompile sweeps any expired entities as a side effect. No user has ever called EXPIRE on Braga.
processExpire is just a synonym for delete (store/ops.go:254-258)
ExpiryLifecycle.t.sol::test_nonOwnerCanExpire confirms EXPIRE has no owner check
The system depositor sweeps expired entities every L1Block update. You don't need to build a 'garbage collector' — the chain has one.

topics.query: What is the maximum lifespan an Arkiv entity can have, and why?
topics.gold: Effective max btl approximately 272 years (u32 cap on btl and expiresAt, 2-second blocks). No MAX_BTL guard anywhere — only Solidity 0.8 checked-arithmetic overflow on currentBlock + btl. No SDK validates upper bound.
distractors: BlockNumber32 = uint32. Comment: 'uint32 overflows at block ~4.3 billion — ~272 years at 2s blocks, ~136 years at 1s blocks (L2). Sufficient for any foreseeable chain.'
No upper-bound validation. fromYears(270) succeeds; fromYears(300) overflows the u32 wire encoding.
u32 overflow at ~272 years is far beyond any product timeline. The architectural cap is not a real constraint.
requirePositiveBtl(btl) reverts ZeroBtl if btl == 0. Only btl check in the entire system.

topics.query: How much does it cost to create an entity on Arkiv Braga, and is there a storage fee?
topics.gold: CREATEs cost ~29,000 gas flat. No per-byte-per-block storage fee. Until the fee model lands, you can be generous with your hot tier. Long btls are free.
distractors: There is no fee model. Storage-service architecture doc section 9 says 'Status: unresolved.' CREATEs cost ~29k gas flat.
Real CREATE gas cost: ~29,000 gas (flat, no per-byte-per-block scaling)
The legacy spec entity-registry-spec.md describes pricePerBytePerBlock x bytes x btl paid in GLM via transferFrom — but this code is not in the deployed contract.
Fee model pricePerBytePerBlock x bytes x btl paid in GLM via transferFrom is not implemented; CREATE is flat ~29k gas

topics.query: What compression ratio does 1-bit RaBitQ achieve on float32 embeddings?
topics.gold: fp32 to 1 bit/dim = 32x raw compression.
distractors: Per-vector overhead: residual norm (fp16, 2 B) + factor (fp16, 2 B) + centroid id (already in IVF). At D = 1024: 128 + 4 = 132 B vs 4096 B = ~31x effective.
1-bit per dim, ~32x compression, 95%+ recall with SQ8 refine.
1-bit RaBitQ at 768-d compresses 32x at ~94% recall, ~95-99% with SQ8 refine.
1:32 compression (fp32 to 1 bit)

topics.query: What is RaBitQ's theoretical error bound and why is it better than PQ?
topics.gold: RaBitQ's headline contribution is an unbiased estimator of inner-product / squared L2 with a sharp, distribution-free error bound of order O(1/sqrt(D)), while matching or beating PQ in practice.
distractors: The dominant prior art — Product Quantization (PQ) and its variants (OPQ, ScaNN's anisotropic PQ) — has no theoretical error bound. Errors are data-dependent and unpredictable, especially on out-of-distribution queries.
Asymptotic optimality. Error decays as O(2^(-B) / sqrt(D)), matching the information-theoretic lower bound for B-bit codes on the unit sphere.
The O(1/sqrt(D)) rate is asymptotically optimal for any D-bit code (matches the Alon-Klartag FOCS 2017 lower bound for the geometric problem).
LSH (sign random projections) is 1-bit, O(1/sqrt(D)) for cosine, popcount(XOR), unbiased but worse constants than RaBitQ

topics.query: Why does RaBitQ apply a random rotation before quantizing?
topics.gold: After rotation, the quantization residual of any data vector behaves like that of a uniformly random unit vector. The error becomes a function of dimension only, not of data distribution.
distractors: Before quantizing, apply a uniformly random orthogonal matrix P (sampled once, shared dataset-wide)
In practice P is implemented as a Fast Hadamard Transform (FHT, O(D log D)) rather than a dense DxD matmul.
P is a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style isometry: preserves L2 norm and inner product exactly, but randomizes the alignment between data and codebook.
K outlier channels. Rotation actually helps — it spreads outliers across channels (same reason QuaRot/SpinQuant use Hadamard rotations).

topics.query: Can I use the NTU RaBitQ library directly for KV cache compression?
topics.gold: KV cache compression (compressing the per-layer K/V tensors that attention reads each token) is Tier B — RaBitQ-the-idea fits; RaBitQ-the-library does not.
distractors: Built for static IVF/HNSW indexes, not [B, H, T, d_head] streaming tensors that grow every decoded token.
No GPU attention kernel — FastScan is CPU-SIMD.
Memory / retrieval layer is Tier A, ship it
Model weight compression is Tier C — wrong tool. Use GPTQ / AWQ / SpinQuant / QuaRot.

topics.query: How many ERCs are in Cortex's final ship-now stack and which are they?
topics.gold: After 4 specialist briefs + red-team adversarial review, the ship list collapses from 15+ proposed standards to six.
distractors: Explicitly dead (cut for concrete technical reasons, not aesthetics): ERC-7857, ERC-6551, ERC-2535 (Diamonds), ERC-4337, ERC-7702 (until Braga tx-type-4 is verified), ERC-2771, ERC-7715/7710, ERC-8001, ERC-8183, ERC-7683.
Total ERC-touching budget: ~10 person-days (excluding the relayer service and SQLite daemon, which are application code, not ERC integration).
The convergent picks across multiple agents that survived: EIP-712, ERC-1271, ERC-6492, ERC-4361 (SIWE).
Corpus: /docs/ERC/erc-knowledge-base/ — 583 ERCs indexed

topics.query: What role does ERC-5169 scriptURI play in Cortex's sovereignty story?
topics.gold: The SynapseAgentRegistry contract publishes scriptURI = ['ipfs://...synapse-mirror.js', 'https://github.com/.../synapse-mirror.js'] pointing to the SQLite-mirror replay logic.
distractors: ERC-5169 (Final, TokenScript ecosystem) lets a contract publish URIs to a client-side replay script.
ERC-5169 turns our SQLite-mirror script into a chain-native recovery artifact, which is the sovereignty pitch made concrete.
The pitch's Pillar 4 is the local SQLite event mirror — the 'data sovereignty' story.
ERC-5169 (scriptURI), Final: Tiny Synapse registry points at the SQLite-mirror replay script — chain-native sovereignty proof

topics.query: Why was ERC-7857 (AI Agents NFT with private metadata) cut from the stack?
topics.gold: Every transfer path requires a TransferValidityProof validated by a TEE oracle or ZK verifier configured per AttestationConfig[]. No such oracle exists on Arkiv Braga.
distractors: DEAD: Requires TransferValidityProof[] via TEE oracle; no TEE oracle deployed on Braga; stub verifier is a credibility liability
Shipping with a stub verifier that always returns true is worse than not shipping the ERC at all — any judge familiar with 7857 will see through it instantly. Cut.
ERC-6551: Smart-contract account can't speak brotli-RLP to the precompile; transferable-mind story isn't in the pitch

Additionally: ERC-7857 conflates agent identity (which ERC-8004 already does) with encrypted metadata transfer (which Synapse doesn't need — Arkiv attributes are public-by-default and queryable, that's the point).

topics.query: How does Cortex use ERC-8004 — as a dependency or something else?
topics.gold: The resolution: mimic the event shape, take no hard dependency.
distractors: A 50-line SynapseAgentRegistry.sol (Ownable, no proxy) emits these events with this exact shape
Why this works: if ERC-8004 wins, the events already match — any 8004 indexer reads Synapse for free. If competing ERC-8122 wins, swap the registry implementation; events are cheap.
Risk: ERC-8004 is 9 months old, Draft, with a competing draft (ERC-8122 by Prem Makeig, created 2025-12-17). Two competing drafts is not a converged ecosystem.
ERC-8004 event-shape mimicry (not dependency), Draft: Emit Registered/MetadataSet events from a 50-line custom registry — forward-compatible if 8004 wins, free to swap if 8122 wins

topics.query: What does ERC-5792 wallet_getCapabilities let the dapp do?
topics.gold: wallet_getCapabilities lets the dapp probe what the user's wallet actually supports — atomic batch, paymaster sponsorship, session-key issuance — and degrade gracefully.
distractors: ERC-1271 (Final, universal smart-wallet adoption) handles smart-contract signers; ERC-6492 (Final) handles counterfactual smart wallets that haven't been deployed yet
The novelty in the stack is pairing it with ERC-5267 (eip712Domain()), Final since 2022, so wallets and downstream tools can discover Synapse's domain
ERC-5792 (wallet_sendCalls / getCapabilities), Final: Feature-detect what user's wallet can batch/sponsor; degrade gracefully
The red-team agent flagged this as the single most-important ERC that all four specialists missed.

topics.query: What citation thresholds promote a memory from working to episodic to semantic?
topics.gold: Promotion thresholds: working to episodic at 2 citations, episodic to semantic at 5 citations across 3 distinct sessions
distractors: Initial working-tier lifespan: 1 hour (was 6h originally — shortened for fee-model defense)
Semantic-tier lifespan: 1 year (was 5y — same reason)
Reinforcement: +24h per citation, accumulative not REPLACE
Citation must validate against last recall set (getLastRecallIds()) — drops hallucinated cites

topics.query: How does Cortex keep memories encrypted at rest while still allowing recall?
topics.gold: Design decision: decrypt-at-recall (NOT in the daemon) so the mirror is ciphertext-at-rest too (laptop theft is not a memory leak), daemon + MMR untouched (payload_hash over on-chain ciphertext is key-independent — a no-key daemon still anchors correctly), and the wallet is load-bearing exactly where the negative control needs it.
distractors: src/lib/payload-key.ts (NEW) — memoized wallet-derived AES key from CORTEX_USER_SIGNATURE
src/lib/crypto.ts — HKDF-derived AES-256-GCM keys from user wallet signature
src/constants.ts — SEALED_CONTENT_TYPE = 'application/x-cortex-sealed'.
src/lib/batch-writer.ts — createMemory/createMemories seal RaBitQ/rule bytes + stamp SEALED_CONTENT_TYPE. Non-memory writes (citation/state_root/market) stay plaintext.

topics.query: What does the Cortex MMR accumulator actually commit to?
topics.gold: MMR commits to set inclusion, NOT nearest-neighbor (KZG vector commitments deferred to v2)
distractors: State accumulator that turns Arkiv from 'raw memory storage' into 'verifiable state registry'. Local MMR singleton built from SQLite on boot; appended on every hydrate.
Hash function: keccak256 (matches EVM precompile + future Arkiv anchors)
Every act() decision now broadcasts the current MMR root to Arkiv as a state_root entity — one cheap commitment per agent decision, regardless of how many memories changed.
Trustless KNN (KZG vector commitments) is deferred — MMR commits to set inclusion only

topics.query: How does Cortex split entity ownership between session key and user wallet?
topics.gold: Hierarchical ownership: creator = sessionKey, owner = userEOA
distractors: src/lib/ownership.ts — promoteOwnership for tier-promotion ownership transfer
cortexQuery({createdBy: SESSION_KEY}) is the trusted-read pattern (arkiv-best-practices section 12)
v1 trust model: backend relayer holds session-key EOA, bounded by SessionAuthorization
If transferability is ever needed, the right primitive is Arkiv's native TRANSFER op (op code 4 in arkiv-contracts/src/lib.rs) — change ownership of the entity directly.

topics.query: How many bytes does Cortex use to store one compressed 1536-d embedding?
topics.gold: 1-bit Rotational Quantization. The math pillar — 1536-d float32 embeddings compressed to 198 bytes per memory (31x compression ratio).
distractors: 198-byte pack: 192 sign bytes + 2 norm fp16 + 2 align fp16 + 2 reserved (centroid u16)
Pad input to 2048-d before Hadamard rotation (deterministic), quantize first 1536 dims
RaBitQ tile 1536d to 198B 31x
Self-recall scores: 0.9998 - 1.0002 (essentially 1.0 for unit vectors)

topics.query: Can I deploy a custom Solidity escrow contract on Braga for the memory market?
topics.gold: Conclusion: Braga's public RPC only admits transactions directed at the Arkiv precompile. General EVM deployment (and even ordinary transfers) are not exposed.
distractors: Every eth_sendRawTransaction for a non-precompile tx is rejected with -32602 'Missing or invalid parameters'
The on-chain memory market cannot be built on Braga — there is no contract to deploy escrow (ERC-8183) or SynapticMarket.sol into. contracts/*.sol in this repo are aspirational.
eth_estimateGas works even for contract creation (56132) — the op-reth EVM will simulate a deploy. So the limit is not the EVM engine.
Yet SDK mutateEntities lands every time — because it always sends to the Arkiv precompile ARKIV_ADDRESS with value: 0n + brotli-RLP data
